infobox.title: Privacy Stack
infobox.imageCaption: GL.iNet router with AdGuard Home running network-wide DNS filtering

lead:
My **Privacy Stack** is a layered personal privacy and security setup spanning OS hardening, browser fingerprint resistance, hardware-level network filtering, self-hosted services, and planned email migration. It currently sits at approximately **85% completeness** — the main remaining gap is Gmail, which I haven't yet migrated to a privacy-respecting provider.

I've built the entire stack on free and open source software, which connects philosophically to [[foss-coin|FOSS Coin]] and the broader FOSS-first approach documented in my [[about|profile]]. Every layer is either already deployed or has a concrete plan for deployment through my [[homelab|Libre Cloud homelab]].

content.content:
The OS layer is the foundation. Running **Arch Linux via [[linux-setup|Omarchy]]** gives me the most control over what software is installed and what runs in the background. Hyprland Wayland tiling WM has minimal background services by design — compared to a typical desktop environment, the attack surface and telemetry footprint are dramatically reduced.

Key decisions I made at this layer:
- **Chromium removed** (telemetry) — replaced with LibreWolf
- **Spotify removed** (tracking) — no replacement
- **MAC address randomisation** enabled on all network interfaces
- **Fork control**: I maintain a personal GitHub fork of Omarchy to selectively apply upstream changes on my own terms

content.title: My trust model
content.content: Arch packages flow through the Arch pacman ecosystem, not DHH's servers. Even using Omarchy, the underlying package signatures and build infrastructure remain the Arch Linux project's — not a third party's.

I use **LibreWolf** — a Firefox fork with hardened anti-fingerprinting defaults applied out of the box. Key differences from stock Firefox that I care about:
- `resistFingerprinting` enabled by default — canvas, WebGL, audio, timezone, and font fingerprinting are all blocked
- No telemetry or crash reporting sent to Mozilla
- Stricter cookie defaults and cross-site isolation

**uBlock Origin** runs on top of LibreWolf in medium mode — blocking all third-party scripts and frames by default, with manual allowlist rules I've added over time for sites that require scripts to function.

I also explored Firefox Multi-Account Containers for session isolation per site category (banking in one container, social media in another, shopping in another).

content.title: Known quirk — dark theme
content.content: LibreWolf's resistFingerprinting setting can break dark mode rendering on some sites. Fix: toggle resistFingerprinting off and on in about:config to restore correct theming. I've hit this a few times.
The DNS layer is the most technically layered part of my stack — I run multiple filtering systems at different levels:

rows.Scope: AdGuard Home on home subnet — blocks ads/malware for all devices on my home network
Ads + Trackers + Malware filtering on my laptop and phone
Planned — will encrypt traffic from device to homelab exit node

content.title: College WiFi constraint
content.content: SonicWALL SSL inspection on the college WiFi blocks pacman package downloads. My workaround: mobile hotspot for system updates on campus.

rows.Status: ❌ Pending — my main gap. Custom domain contact@lingadevaru.in active.
✅ Live — no fingerprinting, no Google Analytics on any site I own
